perturbation.original_text: AT&T shall pay Vendor no later than sixty (60) days after receipt of such monthly payment request for Structured Payments. AT&T shall pay, or provide a written notice of any good faith dispute to, Vendor no later than ninety (90) days after receipt of an invoice for Maintenance Fees.
perturbation.changed_text: AT&T shall pay Vendor no later than one hundred and twenty (120) days after receipt of such monthly payment request for Structured Payments. AT&T shall pay, or provide a written notice of any good faith dispute to, Vendor no later than one hundred and eighty (180) days after receipt of an invoice for Maintenance Fees.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes payment deadlines of 60 and 90 days, respectively. Lengthening these periods to 120 and 180 days creates an inconsistency because payment terms exceeding standard durations may violate prompt payment laws or industry norms, effectively delaying vendor compensation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay contractors within 30 days of receiving a proper invoice. The modified text extends these payment periods far beyond this limit, thus contradicting the Prompt Payment Act's intent to ensure timely payments to contractors.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends payment deadlines to 120 and 180 days, while the Law Explanation indicates that the Prompt Payment Act generally requires payment within 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (31 USC 3903) also supports this by stating regulations shall provide that the required payment date is 30 days after a proper invoice is received. The extended deadlines in the modified text directly contradict this 30-day requirement, creating a clear non-compliance with the Prompt Payment Act's intent for timely payments.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall maintain complete and accurate records relating to the Work and the performance of this Agreement. AT&T and its auditors (including internal audit staff and external auditors) and governmental authorities shall have the right to review such records ("AT&T Audits") held and created by Vendor, to verify the following:
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall maintain complete and accurate records relating to the Work and the performance of this Agreement for one year. AT&T and its auditors (including internal audit staff and external auditors) and governmental authorities shall have the right to review such records ("AT&T Audits") held and created by Vendor, to verify the following:
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record maintenance period to one year is inconsistent with typical accounting and legal requirements, as many laws necessitate retaining records for several years.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 516.2 requires employers to preserve payroll records for at least three years. Limiting the record-keeping duration to one year directly contradicts this regulation under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: eCFR :: 29 CFR 516.2 -- Employees subject to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions pursuant to section 6 or sections 6 and 7(a) of the Act. perturbation.justification: The changed text limits record maintenance to one year, while the Law Explanation states that 29 CFR § 516.2 requires employers to preserve payroll records for at least three years. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing eCFR :: 29 CFR 516.2, implies a legal obligation to maintain records related to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions. The reduction to one year directly contradicts this regulation under the FLSA, as mentioned in the Law Explanation. This qualifies as an Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because the time-sensitive obligation in the changed text does not align with legal requirements, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor will maintain and retain the records set forth in Subsection (a) during the term of the Agreement and for three (3) years thereafter (unless a discovery or legal hold request is made with respect to such records, in which case Vendor shall retain such records until AT&T notifies Vendor that such discovery or legal hold request has expired).
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor will maintain and retain the records set forth in Subsection (a) during the term of the Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter (unless a discovery or legal hold request is made with respect to such records, in which case Vendor shall retain such records until AT&T notifies Vendor that such discovery or legal hold request has expired).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the retention period of records to one year creates a conflict with standard regulatory and legal expectations which often require longer retention periods to accommodate audits, legal claims, and other oversight activities.
perturbation.law_explanation: I.R.C. § 6501 generally provides for a three-year statute of limitations for assessing tax. Reducing the record retention period to one year means that relevant documents could be destroyed before the IRS’s assessment period ends, potentially hindering audits and creating non-compliance. The official IRS website states this three year assessment period. This directly conflicts with the one year retention.

Limitations on assessment and collection (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time aft
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 6501 - Limitations on assessment and collection U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) General rule Except as otherwise provided in this section, the amount of any tax imposed by this title shall be assessed within 3 years after the return was filed (whether or not such return was filed on or after the date prescribed) or, if the tax is payable by stamp, at any time after such tax became due and before the expiration of 3 years after the date on which any part of such tax was paid, and no proceeding in court without assessment for the collection of such tax shall be begun after the expiration of such period. For purposes of this chapter, the term “ return ” means the return required to be filed by the taxpayer (and does not include a return of any person from whom the taxpayer has received an item of income, gain, loss, deduction, or credit). (b) Time return deemed filed (1) Early return For purposes of this section, a return of tax imposed by this title, except tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24, filed before the last day prescribed by law or by regulations promulgated pursuant to law for the filing thereof, shall be considered as filed on such last day. (2) Return of certain employment and withholding taxes For purposes of this section, if a return of tax imposed by chapter 3, 4, 21, or 24 for any period ending with or within a calendar year is filed before April 15 of the succeeding calendar year, such return shall be considered filed on April 15 of such calendar year. (3) Return executed by Secretary Notwithstanding the provisions of paragraph (2) of section 6020(b), the execution of a return by the Secretary pursuant to the authority conferred by such section shall not start the running of the period of limitations on assessment and collectio
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the record retention period to one year, while I.R.C. § 6501, as stated in both scraped snippets, sets a three-year statute of limitations for assessing tax. The Law Explanation highlights that destroying documents before the IRS's assessment period ends creates non-compliance. The original text ensures a three-year retention, aligning with the IRS requirement. The change directly undermines this alignment, creating a contradiction. Therefore, the modified clause fails to meet legal standards for tax assessment periods.
